Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
Off Calendar Feb-04-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. NICHOLAS PAULOS, Esq. of Prindle, Goetz, Barnes & Reinholtz LLP (Tel: 562-436-3946) for Cross-Defendant Alfa Private Security Services, Inc. (sued as Doe 1)
Atty. JAMES J. ORLAND, Esq. of Orland Law Group (Tel: 310-492-5705) for Defendants/Cross Complainants 1233 Polk Street, LLC dba Mayes Oyster House and Polk Street Hospitality, LLC.

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT ALFA PRIVATE SECURITY SERVICES, INC.'s Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant and Cross-Defendant Alfa Private Security Services, Inc.'s motion for determination of good faith settlement is granted. Plaintiffs have agreed to dismiss their complaint against Alfa with prejudice for a waiver of costs. Movant presents evidence that it lacks any substantial exposure to liability in this case because it had no contract with defendant 1233 Polk Street LLC dba Mayes Oyster House to provide security on the date of the incident in question. Mr. Ieremia, the security guard, testified unequivocally that "Alfa Private Security Services, Inc. was not performing any security guard services that night at Mayes Oyster House." The evidence also includes uncontradicted testimony that Matt Corvi, Mayes' owner, paid Mr. Ieremia (not Alfa) in cash for the security services, that no contract between Mayes and Alfa for the period in question was ever finalized or agreed upon, and testimony by both Mr. Ieremia and Alfa that Mr. Ieremia lacked authority to bind Alfa to a security guard contract without the permission of its owner, Mr. Ferooz Nangeyali. Further, the record before the Court strongly suggests that Mayes' owner, Matt Corvi, was complicit-several months after the incident-in fabricating a back-dated "contract" with Alfa in a transparent attempt to defraud the parties and the court. Mr. Corvi's testimony regarding his purported "belief" or "assumption" that he had hired Alfa to provide security fails to explain away or even address any of that evidence. "[I]f the reason for 'cleaning up' the pleadings is plaintiff's belief that the settling defendant bears minimal or no liability for the plaintiff's injuries, a good faith motivation may still be found. . . . No purpose is served by requiring a plaintiff to expend energy and resources to continue with a suit against a party it views as nonculpable." (Ex-Cell-O Corp. v. Holdener (1986) 181 Cal.App.3d 25, 33 [affirming dismissal of cross-complaint against settling individual defendant after plaintiff agreed to dismiss the complaint against him in exchange for a waiver of costs and an agreement not to sue for malicious prosecution].) The party asserting the lack of good faith has the burden of proof on that issue (Code Civ. Proc. Section 877.6(d)). Under the circumstances, objector Mayes Oyster House fails to carry its burden.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
